title: Turns Out 'Saying The Quiet Part Out Loud' Is A Whole Dating Trend Now
trend: "Clear-coding" — 2026 dating journalism's term for stating intentions upfront ("I want a real date, not a link-up") instead of vibing about it for weeks. We tie it to a real, shipped product step, not a roadmap promise.
hook: Dating trend pieces are currently very excited that people are 'clear-coding' — just saying what they actually want. We've had a word for that for a while. We call it sign-up.
slides: Apparently there's a name now for 'just saying what you actually want.' It's called clear-coding. 👇
For years the move was: vague bio, vaguer texts, hope the other person 'just gets it' eventually.
Clear-coding is the opposite — 'I want a real date, not a link-up' energy, upfront, no decoding required.
We didn't wait for the trend piece. Every KeepItUp game asks one thing before you request a spot: dating, friendship, or community — pick honestly, see who else picked the same.
No decoder ring required. No 'so what are we' conversation three weeks in.
Turns out being direct was always going to catch on eventually. We just started there.
Open worldwide. Early access — link in bio. 🎾
caption: Dating writers just discovered that stating your actual intentions upfront works better than hoping someone reads between the lines — they're calling it 'clear-coding.' We didn't wait for the trend piece: every KeepItUp game asks your intention (dating, friendship, or community) before you request a spot, honestly, no decoding required. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Comment which intention you'd pick first — dating, friendship, or community — there's no wrong answer.
imageConcept: 7-card carousel, 4:5, anthracite (#20262B) background throughout, off-white (#F1F5F3) body text. Cards 2-3 use a muted grey-on-anthracite 'vague/blurry' text treatment (representing the old vague-bio era). Card 4 hard-cuts to full bold neon-green (#3BEA7E) highlight background with off-white text as the tone flips from 'old way' to 'us' — a small checkbox-style icon (three ticked options: dating/friendship/community) sits beside the text. Final card: KeepItUp wordmark centered, neon-green 'link in bio' arrow, off-white 'open worldwide' micro-copy.

title: Apparently 'Yearning' Is Back. We'd Argue It Never Left Match Point.
trend: 2026 dating coverage says 'yearning' — slow anticipation over instant access — is the new cultural mood. We compare the bad kind of anticipation (waiting on a reply) to the good kind (a close match) and land on ours.
hook: Dating trend reports say 'yearning' is back in style. Bold claim from people who've clearly never been down 5-6 in a tiebreak.
slides: Dating culture just rediscovered anticipation. They're calling it 'yearning.' 👇
Turns out humans like a little tension before the payoff. Genuinely groundbreaking research.
Meanwhile, waiting three days for a reply has just been quietly called 'the algorithm' this whole time. Same tension, worse ending.
Here's tension that actually resolves: down a set, one more rally, everything on the line for about four seconds.
That's the good kind of yearning. It ends. You find out. Nobody leaves you on read for it.
We can't manufacture chemistry. We can absolutely manufacture a tiebreak.
Open worldwide. Early access — link in bio. 🎾
caption: Dating trend reports say 2026 is bringing back 'yearning' — slow anticipation over instant access. We'd argue the good version of that feeling already exists, it's just called a tiebreak: real tension, and it actually resolves. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Comment the sport that gives you the best kind of nerves — we're using the replies to shape what we open next.
imageConcept: 7-card carousel, 4:5, anthracite background. Cards 2-3 styled with a slow-fade/low-contrast text treatment suggesting drawn-out waiting. Card 4-5 shift to a scoreboard-style tension motif (bold neon-green tiebreak-score graphic, e.g. '6-6') as the tone turns from 'app anticipation' to 'match anticipation.' Final card: KeepItUp wordmark centered, neon-green 'link in bio' arrow.

title: Guess Which Era This First Meeting Happened In
trend: Recreates the structural mechanic of the viral "guess which generation took the pic" carousel trend (own copy/art only, no borrowed images) as a 'guess the era' first-meeting story.
hook: Same first-meeting story, five different eras. Guess which one before the last slide. 👀
slides: Same first-meeting story, told five different ways. Guess the era before you reach the last slide. 👇
Era 1: the village fair. Someone's aunt is definitely involved. The whole street knows by Sunday.
Era 2: the newspaper personal ad. 'Enjoys long walks, will pay by the word, so that's genuinely all you get to know.'
Era 3: the public Top 8. Ranking your crush for the entire internet to see, zero context, zero shame.
Era 4: the 41-paragraph dating-app bio. A full personality assessment required before a single reply.
Era 5: Tuesday, 7pm, court 3. Nobody wrote an essay. Everybody just showed up.
Guess which one's ours — it's the shortest bio you'll ever need: a time and a place.
Open worldwide. Early access — link in bio to write your own era 5. 🎾
caption: Every generation has had its own version of 'how people met' — the fair, the personal ad, the Top 8, the 41-paragraph bio. Guess which one's ours before the last slide (spoiler: it's the shortest one). Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Comment which era you guessed before you got to the reveal — and which one your parents would swear by.
imageConcept: 8-card carousel, 4:5, anthracite background. Each 'era' card carries a small era-appropriate icon rendered in our own simple flat-icon style (never a real photo) — a bunting/fair-flag icon, a folded-newspaper icon, a numbered-list icon, a long-scroll-text icon — building visual variety per era. Final two cards flip to bold neon-green with a running-figure/racket icon and the KeepItUp wordmark, off-white 'link in bio.'

title: The Line From Your Couch To Actually Showing Up
trend: Recreates the structural mechanic of the trending 'follow the line' carousel (a continuous line design device connecting every slide) — our own line, our own copy, no borrowed art.
hook: There's a very specific line between 'I should get out more' and actually doing it. Follow it. 👇
slides: There's a line between 'I should get out more' and actually doing it. Follow it. 👇
It starts on the sofa, several tabs open, several apps open, nothing open enough to actually leave the house.
It loops through a group chat that has 'planned' the same hangout for four straight months.
It dips through a bio you've rewritten nine times, hoping honesty and marketing can somehow be the same sentence.
Then it just... goes outside. A court. A trail. A climbing wall. Nothing to write, nowhere to hide.
The line was never complicated. It was just long, because every step except the last one was optional.
Skip to the last step. Open worldwide — link in bio. 🎾
caption: There's a very specific, very long line between 'I should get out more' and actually doing it — most of it is optional. We just make the last step (show up, nothing to write, nowhere to hide) the easiest one. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Save this and actually follow your own line somewhere real this week.
imageConcept: 7-card carousel, 4:5, anthracite background. A continuous neon-green (#3BEA7E) line runs across all cards — exiting each slide's right edge and re-entering the next slide's left edge at the same vertical height, tracing a path that visually loops through 'sofa,' 'group chat,' 'bio' motifs (small flat icons the line passes through) before straightening out into a clean diagonal on the final two cards toward the KeepItUp wordmark and 'link in bio' arrow.

title: This Is Your Sign (The Non-Ominous Kind)
trend: Rides the extremely common, low-production 'this is your sign to ___' meta-format as a single-card meme — cheap, native, high tag/share rate.
hook: This is your sign that 'we should hang out sometime' quietly expired a while ago.
caption: Not a scary sign. Just a mildly overdue one. If your 'we should hang out sometime' thread is still technically alive but hasn't moved in months, this is it. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Tag whoever needs this sign more than you do.
imageConcept: Single 4:5 image, anthracite background. Large bold neon-green headline centered: 'THIS IS YOUR SIGN' with a small hand-drawn-style arrow/starburst icon (illustrated, not a real photo) pointing at off-white subtext below: 'that ‘we should hang out sometime’ expired around week six and nobody sent the memo.' KeepItUp mark small, bottom-right corner, 'open worldwide' micro-copy beneath it.

title: The Sound Every Bad Scroll Session Deserves
trend: Recreates the structure and timing of the viral 'saxophone gets louder' dramatic-escalation format — our own royalty-free horn-sting sound effect, never the meme's original audio/clip, never a real person.
hook: 0:00 — a totally normal Tuesday-night scroll. By 0:08, it is not.
script:
0:00-0:02 — Static shot: a phone screen (mocked, generic profile-card UI, no real app logo/names), thumb scrolling calmly. On-screen text: 'a completely normal tuesday night.' No music yet, just ambient room tone.
0:02-0:04 — Scroll speeds up slightly. On-screen text counter starts: 'match #1... nice smile, no bio.' A single soft horn note begins, low, almost unnoticeable (our own original stinger, not the meme's clip).
0:04-0:06 — Scroll faster. On-screen text: 'match #2... six-photo carousel, zero personality.' Horn note climbs one step, gets a little louder, a little more insistent.
0:06-0:08 — Scroll faster still, thumb visibly tensing. On-screen text: 'match #3... it's your ex's flatmate.' Horn hits its loudest, most dramatic note — hard freeze-frame on the person's face, comedic shock.
0:08-0:11 — Hard cut to total silence and daylight: same person, mid-laugh, sweaty, having clearly just finished a padel/run/climb session with someone new. On-screen text: 'meanwhile, this took 45 minutes and involved exactly one (1) awkward introduction.'
0:11-0:15 — On-screen text only, no talking: 'no scroll. no counter. no ex's flatmate. just a time and a place.'
0:15-0:19 — End card: KeepItUp motion-arc mark on anthracite, on-screen text 'meet through movement — open worldwide, early access,' caption-only line: 'link in bio before match #4 gets weirder.'
caption: There's a very specific sound effect a Tuesday-night scroll session deserves right around match #3. We gave it one. Then we showed you the 45-minute version that doesn't need it. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Duet this with the exact scroll-to-cringe moment that deserves its own dramatic horn note.
imageConcept: Cover/thumbnail frame: anthracite background, bold neon-green on-screen text 'match #3...' with a small comedic freeze-frame-style shock icon (illustrated, not a real face), off-white subtext 'you know the sound.', KeepItUp mark small bottom-right corner.

title: New Entry In The Dating Dictionary
trend: Dating journalism keeps coining new terms for old feelings ('yearning,' 'wildflowering,' 'clear-coding'). Fresh structural format: a mock dictionary-entry card, self-aware meta-commentary that we're contributing our own term.
hook: Dating writers keep naming feelings we already had words for. We're adding one to the pile.
caption: 2026's dating dictionary keeps growing — 'yearning,' 'wildflowering,' 'clear-coding.' Cool, we're adding one: the act of ending a three-week text thread by just... showing up to something real. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Comment your own made-up word for this feeling — the best one gets its own dictionary card next round.
imageConcept: Single 4:5 image, anthracite background, styled like a clean dictionary entry card: off-white serif-look word at top 'show·up·ping', small neon-green '(verb)' tag beside it, off-white definition text below: 'the act of ending a three-week text thread by just... showing up to something real. See also: revolutionary.' KeepItUp mark small, bottom-right corner, 'open worldwide' micro-copy.

title: Finish The Sentence: 'I'd Actually Show Up If...'
trend: Genuinely new experiment (the ~20% new-bet slot) — first test of a low-effort 'finish the sentence' comment mechanic distinct from the comment-to-win / tag-a-friend mechanics already running, to demand-map sport/time/city preferences.
hook: Finish the sentence in the comments. We're actually reading these. 👇
slides: Finish the sentence. We're actually reading the comments on this one. 👇
'I'd actually show up if it were ___.'
Padel? Running? Climbing? 6am or 9pm? Solo or with a friend tagging along? All valid answers.
This isn't a rhetorical question — your answers are genuinely shaping which sports and cities we open next.
Comment your ending. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio. 🎾
caption: Finish the sentence: 'I'd actually show up if it were ___.' Not rhetorical — we're reading every reply and using them to decide what we open next. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Comment your ending to 'I'd actually show up if...' — sport, time, whatever's true for you.
imageConcept: 5-card carousel, 4:5, anthracite background. Card 2 renders the sentence with a visible blank/underline in neon-green where the reader mentally fills it in, off-white text otherwise. Card 3 uses a simple icon row (racket, running-shoe, climbing-hold) to prompt options without dictating an answer. Final card: KeepItUp wordmark centered, neon-green 'link in bio' arrow, off-white 'open worldwide, early access.'
